definition: A support vector machine (SVM) is a binary classifier that finds a separating hyperplane wᵀx + b = 0 with the largest geometric margin between classes. Training points that constrain the solution — on or inside the margin — are support vectors. With soft margin, the regularization parameter C trades off margin width against hinge-loss penalties for misclassified or margin-violating points. This topic is the canonical home for max-margin classification, support vectors, and soft-margin C in classical supervised ML.
layman: An SVM draws the widest possible street between two groups of points and uses only the points at the curb — the support vectors — to decide where the street goes. If the data is messy and not perfectly separable, soft margin lets a few points step into the street; C controls how much you punish that versus keeping the street wide.
content:
## Support Vector Machines

A **support vector machine (SVM)** is a **binary classifier** that learns a **linear decision boundary** **wᵀx + b = 0** by **maximizing the margin** — the distance between the boundary and the nearest training points of each class.

This topic is the **canonical home** for:

- **Max-margin classifier** — the optimization goal that picks the separating hyperplane with largest geometric margin
- **Support vectors** — the training examples that lie on or inside the margin and define the boundary
- **Soft margin C** — the regularization knob balancing margin width vs. classification errors

Kernel methods and the full dual formulation live in **Kernels**; distance-to-hyperplane geometry in **Norms & Distances**; probabilistic scoring contrast in **Logistic Regression**; threshold-free evaluation in **ROC & AUC**.

### Decision function

For feature vector **x**, the SVM score is:

**f(x) = wᵀx + b**

Predicted class:

```
class = +1  if f(x) ≥ 0
class = −1  if f(x) < 0
```

(Labels may be encoded as {0, 1} in libraries; the geometric story uses {−1, +1}.)

### Margins

For training point **(xᵢ, yᵢ)** with **yᵢ ∈ {−1, +1}**:

- **Functional margin:** **yᵢ (wᵀxᵢ + b)** — how far the point is on the correct side in score units
- **Geometric margin:** **yᵢ (wᵀxᵢ + b) / ||w||₂** — perpendicular distance to the hyperplane in feature space

**Hard-margin SVM** (linearly separable data) requires **yᵢ (wᵀxᵢ + b) ≥ 1** for all **i** — every point is at least one functional margin unit from the boundary.

The **max-margin** solution minimizes **||w||₂²** (equivalently maximizes geometric margin **2/||w||₂** between parallel margin hyperplanes **wᵀx + b = ±1**).

### Support vectors (canonical definition here)

**Support vectors** are training points that **actively constrain** the optimal hyperplane:

- In the **hard-margin** case: points with **yᵢ (wᵀxᵢ + b) = 1** — they sit on the margin boundary
- In the **soft-margin** case: also points with **yᵢ (wᵀxᵢ + b) < 1** (inside margin or misclassified) when their slack penalty is active

At prediction time, **only support vectors matter** in the dual form **f(x) = Σᵢ αᵢ yᵢ (xᵢᵀx) + b** — most **αᵢ = 0**. That sparsity is a hallmark of SVMs vs. k-NN, which stores the entire training set.

### Soft margin and C (canonical definition here)

Real data is often **not perfectly separable**. **Soft-margin SVM** introduces slack variables **ξᵢ ≥ 0**:

**Constraints:** **yᵢ (wᵀxᵢ + b) ≥ 1 − ξᵢ**

**Objective (primal):** minimize **||w||₂² + C Σᵢ ξᵢ**

| C | Effect |
| --- | --- |
| **Large C** | Heavily penalize slack → narrower margin, fewer training errors, risk of overfitting noise |
| **Small C** | Allow more slack → wider margin, tolerate more violations, smoother boundary |

**C** is **not** a probability; it sets the trade-off between **margin width** and **hinge-loss violations**. Tune **C** (and **γ** for RBF kernels in the **Kernels** topic) via cross-validation.

### Hard vs soft margin

| Setting | When | Behavior |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Hard margin** | Clean, linearly separable data | No slack; any mislabel breaks feasibility |
| **Soft margin** | Noisy or overlapping classes | **C** controls slack; standard in practice |

Libraries almost always use soft margin; **C → ∞** approximates hard margin on separable data.

### Training intuition (beginner)

1. Start with labeled points in feature space.
2. Search for **w**, **b** that separate classes while **maximizing margin**.
3. Identify **support vectors** — the critical training points.
4. For new **x**, compute **f(x) = wᵀx + b** and take the sign.

Optimization is a **convex quadratic program** — a single global optimum (unlike deep nets). Solvers (SMO, liblinear, libsvm) find **w**, **b**, and support-vector indices efficiently.

### Worked 2D sketch

Two classes separated by a line. Points **A** and **B** from opposite classes touch the parallel dashed lines **wᵀx + b = ±1** — they are **support vectors**. Point **C** sits far from the boundary and is **not** a support vector; removing **C** does not change the solution. If **C** were mislabeled inside the margin, **soft margin** with moderate **C** might still find a wide boundary while paying slack cost on **C**.

### Summary map

| Concept | Canonical home |
| --- | --- |
| **Max-margin classifier** | **This topic** |
| **Support vectors** | **This topic** |
| **Soft margin C** | **This topic** |
| Kernel trick / RBF / polynomial kernels | **Kernels** |
| L2 norm / point-to-hyperplane distance | **Norms & Distances** |
| Probabilistic linear classifier (sigmoid + BCE) | **Logistic Regression** |
| Ranking scores with ROC / AUC | **ROC & AUC** |
keyPoints: SVM learns w, b for hyperplane wᵀx + b = 0 by maximizing geometric margin between classes.
Support vectors are training points on or inside the margin that define the boundary; most αᵢ = 0.
Soft-margin objective: minimize ||w||² + C Σξᵢ with yᵢ(wᵀxᵢ + b) ≥ 1 − ξᵢ — C trades margin vs violations.
Prediction: sign(wᵀx + b); f(x) is a score suitable for ranking (see ROC & AUC), not a calibrated probability.
Nonlinear boundaries require feature maps or kernels — see Kernels; this topic covers linear max-margin core.
